title: Inverse-Rendering-Aware Adaptive Density Control for Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting
teaser: Every relightable 3DGS paper observes that vanilla densification spawns Gaussians wherever the rendered RGB residual is high — including residuals caused by shadows, specularities, or material misestimation — leading to "loose geometry" that absorbs shading. Yet none of them changes the densification rule itself: they all keep the original RGB-residual splitter and add normal regularization on top. This project asks whether replacing the densification criterion with an inverse-rendering-aware residual — that distinguishes geometry error from material/lighting error — yields cleaner decompositions and better relighting at no inference cost.
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting's adaptive density control is a heuristic developed for forward novel-view synthesis: it splits or clones Gaussians wherever the photometric residual is large. In an inverse-rendering setting, this heuristic is actively harmful — high residual can mean missing geometric detail, but it can equally mean a baked-in shadow, a poorly explained specular highlight, or a wrong albedo, and densifying in those cases creates Gaussians that absorb shading into geometry. Recent 3DGS densification work (Bulò et al. "Revising Densification", ResGS, opacity-gradient ADC, GeoGaussian) operates entirely in RGB space and ignores this distinction, while every relightable 3DGS pipeline (GS-IR, R3DG, GUS-IR, GeoSplatting, GS-ID) keeps vanilla densification and adds normal regularization to compensate. We propose a decomposition-aware densification rule: at each densification step, the photometric residual is routed through the current PBR estimate plus pretrained 2D priors (normal, intrinsic) to produce a geometry-only residual map, and Gaussians are split only where this geometry residual dominates. The contribution is a drop-in replacement for ADC that improves material decomposition and relighting on FIPT and TensoIR-Synthetic across at least two different relightable 3DGS backbones.
milestones: Reproduce two relightable 3DGS backbones with vanilla densification and diagnose where Gaussians get spawned to explain shadows or specularities.
Decompose the photometric residual into geometry-, material-, and lighting-attributable components using cached 2D priors and the current PBR estimate.
Replace the ADC criterion with a geometry-residual-driven rule and ablate against vanilla and pixel-error-driven densification.
Benchmark relighting fidelity, material-decomposition error, and total Gaussian count across two backbones on FIPT and TensoIR-Synthetic.

prerequisites.label: Advanced Deep Learning for Computer Vision: Visual Computing (ADL4CV)

references.label: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
GS-IR: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Inverse Rendering
GUS-IR: Gaussian Splatting With Unified Shading for Inverse Rendering
Intrinsic Image Diffusion for Indoor Single-view Material Estimation
